rows.v: The house style — blended across years for consistency. Dry. The default Champagne.
Pink, from skin contact or adding red wine. Red-berry notes; food-friendly.
Single great year, aged longer. More depth, more money.
'White from whites' — 100% Chardonnay. Citrus, chalk, finesse.
'White from blacks' — Pinot Noir/Meunier pressed pale. Rounder, fruitier.
A house's top bottling (e.g. the flagship tête de cuvée). Long-aged, age-worthy.
Made by the same estate that grows the grapes (RM on the label). Terroir-driven.
Traditional-method sparkler from Alsace. Champagne method, friendlier price.
Loire traditional-method; Chenin-based, bright and floral.
Burgundy's traditional-method bubbly; Chardonnay/Pinot Noir.
From Limoux, often cited as France's oldest sparkling region.
Limoux's Mauzac-based historic sparkler.
'Pét-Nat' — bottled before first fermentation finishes (méthode ancestrale). Cloudy, funky, trendy.
Tank-method, Glera grape. Light, fruity, floral. The world's casual bubbly.
DOCG Prosecco from Conegliano-Valdobbiadene; finer than basic DOC.
Italy's traditional-method benchmark (Lombardy). Champagne-style depth.
Sweet, low-alcohol, Moscato; fully sparkling ('spumante').
Gently fizzy ('frizzante'), sweet, low-alcohol Moscato.
Red (sometimes rosé) sparkling from Emilia; dry to sweet.
Traditional-method from Trentino, mountain-grown.
Spain's traditional-method sparkler. Champagne method, value pricing.
Top single-estate Cava classification.
Breakaway premium Penedès group; strict organic, hand-harvest rules.
Traditional-method from CA (often by Champagne houses). Ripe-fruited.
Chalk soils like Champagne; crisp, high-acid traditional method.
German sparkling; quality varies from cheap tank to fine traditional.
South Africa's traditional-method sparkling (the local name).
Cool-climate Australian traditional method; rising star.
Second fermentation in the bottle (Champagne, Cava, Franciacorta). Fine, persistent bubbles + autolysis.
Second fermentation in a tank (Prosecco). Fresh, fruity, fast, cheaper.
One fermentation, bottled before it finishes (Pét-Nat). Rustic.
Bottle-fermented then disgorged via tank; a hybrid.
CO₂ injected (cheapest bulk fizz). Large, short-lived bubbles.
The standard dry style (<12 g/L sugar). What most sparkling is.
Confusingly, sweeter than Brut (12–17 g/L).
Bone dry, zero dosage (0–3 g/L). Stark, mineral.
Very dry (0–6 g/L), just above Brut Nature.
Off-dry/medium (17–32 g/L).
Noticeably sweet (32–50 g/L); dessert-friendly.
Sweetest (50+ g/L); rare.
Serve cold (6–9°C); tulip/white-wine glass over a flute to capture aroma.
The sugar-wine added after disgorgement that sets sweetness level.
Yeast breakdown during bottle aging → brioche/bread/toast notes.
Riddling collects the yeast in the neck; disgorgement ejects it.
'Mousse' = the foam; 'bead' = the bubble stream. Fine bead = quality cue.
The classic young-Champagne high notes. Bright, zesty.
Crisp apple and pear — the fruit backbone of most sparkling.
Riper-fruit notes from warmer years or more Pinot.
Delicate floral lift, often in Chardonnay-led wines.
The signature bottle-aged Champagne smell, from autolysis. Tells you it's traditional-method.
Deeper baked, creamy autolytic notes from longer aging.
Quince, marmalade, dried apricot — older, evolved wines.

groups.name: Nutty & Oxidative (Almond, Coffee, Caramel)

rows.v: Long-aged or oxidative styles; mature complexity.

groups.name_es: Frutos secos y oxidativo (almendra, café, caramelo)

rows.v: The mineral/saline 'tension' of great Champagne terroir.

groups.name: Mousse Texture (Fine vs Aggressive)

rows.v: How the bubbles feel — fine creamy mousse = quality; harsh fizz = lesser.

groups.name_es: Textura de la espuma (fina vs. agresiva)

rows.v: TCA musty cardboard, flat sherry-like oxidation, struck-match reduction.

groups.name: Light-Strike & Lost Fizz

rows.v: 'Goût de lumière' from light damage; flatness from a bad cork.

groups.name_es: Golpe de luz y pérdida de burbuja
